TextENGNew: "Yeah, yeah, good work, everyone.@k@vS05/11/100700392. I know it's hot, but hang in there.@k@vS05/11/100700393. If any dump trucks show up for illegal dumping, let us know right away.@k@vS05/11/100700394. We'll be there in a flash."
``Thank you for your cooperation, Ooishi-san.@k@vS05/00/104600051. ...Go ahead.``
A gap just wide enough for a single car was opened in the barricade.
Ooishi gave a short honk -- gratitude, or perhaps a warning -- and drove through the checkpoint.
My appearance this time around was the very picture of suspicion.
A baseball cap, a face mask, and sunglasses.
And on top of that, a hood pulled up despite the sweltering heat. Any self-respecting officer would have been itching to question me.
It was meant as an excessive disguise, given how cautious I needed to be about my face being known...@k...but it may well have been futile already.
Using that resignation as an excuse, I stripped off the unbearably hot hooded windbreaker, pulled off the mask and cap, and tossed them onto the back seat.
``...Oh.@k@vS05/11/100700396. Is that all right?``
``A cheap disguise like this isn't going to help.@k@vS05/13/101300448. They've probably seen through it already.``
``Na ha ha ha.@k@vS05/11/100700398. That just might be the case.``
Ooishi, apparently finding my unexpected nonchalance amusing, kept laughing for a while.
The simple, beautiful scenery of Hinamizawa spread wide before us.
...Without all the complications, what a beautiful village this would be.
...I'd love to bring Yukie here for a drive sometime.
...Come to think of it, I hadn't managed to call her last night...
...I let my thoughts drift along those lines.
But in reality, this village was no peaceful haven.
From the very first briefing at prefectural headquarters, I had been told everything.

At the heart of this village, the Three Families' intelligence network was strengthened to an almost unbelievable degree through blood ties and local connections.@kIt extended not just around Hinamizawa, but across the entire city of Shishibone.
When I first heard that, some part of me hadn't taken it fully seriously.
How could a rural community group possibly have something so grandiose?
...I could no longer deny that, deep down, I had scoffed at the very idea.
But they had not only managed to threaten a government minister, they had demonstrated a complete grasp of our movements -- movements that were supposed to be top secret.
...It was like a small Eastern European nation from a spy novel, run by an all-seeing secret police.
The villagers would not hesitate to use any means necessary, however brutal, if it meant fighting the dam project.
That, too, was something I had been told at prefectural headquarters.
Ah... come to think of it, when I first arrived, hadn't Ooishi himself called this village a war zone?
The residents of this village were not just residents -- they were warriors fighting for the liberation of their homeland.
By definition, they were no different from a militia or guerrilla force.
They were not protected by the Geneva Convention, and in turn, they did not abide by it.
What was unfolding here was a true clandestine struggle -- murkier than any sanctioned battlefield...
I could ride along because Ooishi was with me...@k...but if I stepped out of this car to so much as find a vending machine, there was a real chance I might not come back.
Even without a single bullet flying... this was a battlefield.
No... the only reason bullets weren't flying was that guns were difficult for civilians to obtain in Japan.
So they would arm themselves with whatever weapons were readily available instead.
That was why Ooishi was wearing that stab-proof vest, wasn't it?
``Akasaka-san, are you nervous?@k@vS05/11/100700400. Nfu fu fu!``
My tension was, of course, completely transparent to Ooishi.
If he had already picked up on it, trying to deny or conceal it was pointless.
``...Of course I am.@k@vS05/13/101300451. We're heading into the heart of enemy territory with just the two of us.@k@vS05/13/101300452. This isn't nearly enough manpower for what could go wrong.``
``Something goes wrong?@k@vS05/11/100700402. Na ha ha ha ha ha!@k@vS05/11/100700403. We'll be fine.``
``Isn't every person in this village our enemy?@k@vS05/13/101300454. If they all get their stories straight, a murder in broad daylight here wouldn't leave a shred of evidence.``
This was the essence of the ironclad rule: never barge into a yakuza office alone.
When there was no neutral party in the environment, the normal rules ceased to apply.
Whatever happened, you couldn't prove it, and there was nobody to take your side.
That was the very definition of being deep in the enemy's belly.

"I've told my colleagues that we're heading to the Hinamizawa area.@k@vS05/11/100700405. And in the worst case, we have a radio.@k@vS05/11/100700406. ...Akasaka-san, it's not as easy to pull off a perfect crime as you seem to fear."
``...Well, of course, that doesn't mean we should let our guard down.``
``Akasaka-san, you're wound too tight.@k@vS05/11/100700409. You were far more relaxed the last time we were here together.@k@vS05/11/100700410. Loosen up a little.``
Laughing it off with a booming laugh, Ooishi thumped his chest as if to say 'you can count on me,' and flashed a grin.
``Well, if things really go sideways -- nahahaha!@k@vS05/11/100700412. We've always got this, courtesy of the badge.``
Ooishi made a gesture with his index finger pointed out.@k...Unmistakably miming a gun.
``...Will that actually help?``
``As long as you're not afraid of the aftermath and paperwork, it's actually very reassuring.``
A police officer's gun was really just for show.
Actually firing it required clearing an absurd number of regulations, followed by an agonizingly long ordeal of accountability afterward.
Since the tradeoff was never worth it, officers might draw their weapons, but none of them truly wanted to pull the trigger.
``Akasaka-san, are you carrying one?@k@vS05/11/100700415. One of 'these.'``
``...Well, yes.``
``Ever fired it?``
``I have.@k@vS05/13/101300460. ...In training, though.``
``Well, that figures.@k@vS05/11/100700418. Nfu fu fu...!``
``Then have you, Ooishi-san?``
``Me?@k@vS05/11/100700420. Nahahahaha!@k@vS05/11/100700421. No way -- of course not.``
Wait, he was laughing at me as if he'd had experience, and he hadn't?!@kWe both burst out laughing.

"I've never pointed a gun at anyone either.@k@vS05/11/100700423. I have had guns pointed at me a few times, though.@k@vS05/11/100700424. ...Just staring down a gun barrel is enough to shorten your life by three years, I'd say."
Even if he had never fired a gun,
the breadth of experience Ooishi had was something I couldn't begin to match.
...At that moment, more than ever, I was struck by how reassuring it was to have Ooishi by my side.

``...Come to think of it, I'd rather not have come this way in broad daylight.@k@vS05/11/100700426. But there's no other road for cars to take.@k@vS05/11/100700427. ...Akasaka-san, just to be safe, put the mask back on to hide your face.``
changeReason: Both contain mask instruction; only minor wording differences.

TextENGNew: ``...What's the matter?``
``Can't you hear it over the air conditioning?@k@vS05/11/100700429. I'll turn it off.@k@vS05/11/100700430. Try opening your window.``
When I opened the window, a wave of stifling heat rushed in.
And then... the distant sound of angry shouting reached my ears.
At that moment, the forest on both sides fell away, and our field of vision expanded all at once.
...There it was -- the Hinamizawa Dam construction site.@k...Yes, the very front line of Hinamizawa's war.
``````We are absolutely opposed to the construction of the Hinamizawa Dam!!``````
A crackling, thunderous voice erupted from an enormous loudspeaker.
A roar of angry voices followed in unison, like a crowd stamping the ground as one.
The sheer volume had become a veritable cannon of sound, firing its volleys without cease.
The prefabricated buildings of the site office were enclosed behind double rows of tall fencing, topped with barbed wire.
Lined up in front of those fences stood rows of riot police and their vehicles.
And surrounding them all were the villagers.
Moving as a single living organism, they bellowed their opposition to the dam in unison.
``Oh... there's not too many today.@k@vS05/11/100700432. Lucky us.``
``...That's not too many?``
``No -- when there's a lot of them, the crowd spills all the way onto the road.@k@vS05/11/100700434. You can't even drive through.``
The initial intensity had made me overestimate the numbers, but there were probably only fifty to seventy people at most.
But with six or seven propaganda trucks lined up in formation, their appearance was thoroughly unsettling.
The villagers, too, wore helmets, headbands, and masks covering their faces, amplifying the bizarre atmosphere.
The trucks' massive speakers, after blasting their chorus of slogans, turned the volume up even higher and began broadcasting Buddhist sutras.
The sonic assault was... beyond description.
At this distance it was already this loud... inside the office, the windows were probably rattling in their frames.

``When they really crank it up, it's absolutely unbearable.@k@vS05/11/100700436. The riot squad guys all wear earplugs, and even then their hearing still gets messed up.@k@vS05/11/100700437. You'd last maybe a week before going mad.``
``Can't noise regulations do anything about it?``

``Hmm... this prefecture doesn't actually have noise regulations on the books.@k@vS05/11/100700439. And besides, it's sutras, right?@k@vS05/11/100700440. You can't exactly complain about religious practice.``
At least, I think that's what Ooishi said.
In reality, no matter how tightly we sealed the windows, the thunderous sutras filled the car.
I couldn't even make out what Ooishi, sitting right beside me, was saying.
As the car drew closer to the line of propaganda trucks, the noise became unbearable.
...In the end, plugging my ears with my fingers wasn't enough -- I had to cradle my head in both hands just to keep my skull from splitting apart...
If I had witnessed this terrifying energy sooner, I might have understood the true nature of Hinamizawa Village much earlier.
Now I understood. Their all-or-nothing resolve to protect their homeland at any cost was blindingly clear.
God, so noisy... shut up, shut up, shut up...!!!
My head pounded as though it would crack open, my eardrums buzzed, and on top of it all, nausea was setting in.
Between the sit-in protesters, the propaganda trucks, and the riot police formations and vehicles, the road was anything but easy to navigate.
Even honking the horn to scatter the sit-in protesters proved futile@k-- they, too, wore earplugs and were oblivious to outside sounds, so the car could only inch forward at a crawl.
...Even so, the car eventually pushed through, and we finally escaped the pandemonium.
``...Hey, remember the old man from the mahjong game last night?@k@vS05/11/100700444. You know who I mean?``
I recalled the rough-spoken old man.
The one Ooishi had been calling 'the old man.'
``Yes, I remember.``

``He's the foreman of the main construction site.@k@vS05/11/100700446. Right now, he's probably holed up inside one of those prefabs, seething while he tries to work.@k@vS05/11/100700447. I almost feel sorry for the guy.``
``...If it's like that every day, I don't know how anyone could stand it.``
``Exactly.@k@vS05/11/100700449. People who can't take it anymore keep quitting one after another.@k@vS05/11/100700450. But every time someone quits, the pay goes up. Na ha ha ha!``
changeReason: Old includes 'I won't say it's in vain' (なんとも言えませんが); new drops this nuanced phrase, losing the ambiguous commentary.

TextENGNew: Even accounting for all that noise and hostility, the wages that made Ooishi envious were starting to make sense.
``Dammit, these people...@k@vS05/11/100700453. Can they hear me or can't they...``
A group of sit-in protesters was blocking the road ahead.
Ooishi, growing increasingly irritated, laid on the horn, but they showed no sign of moving.
Could they really not hear the horn over this din?
Or were they hearing it and being deliberately obstructive? There was no way to tell.
If riot police had been nearby, they would have cleared the way, but none happened to be in the area.
``...Ahh, for the love of...@k@vS05/11/100700455. Hang on a moment.``
Ooishi opened his car door.
A blast of heat and even more deafening noise spilled into the car.
Ooishi said something with a grin, but I couldn't make it out.
Something about moving them out of the way, probably.
Ooishi stepped out and closed the door.@kThe noise abated slightly.
I watched blankly as Ooishi approached the seated group and began some sort of exchange.
...Would we find the minister's grandson in the Takatsudo district?
I wanted to find him.
Find him and free myself from the inexplicable grip this village had on me.
I couldn't help but pray for that.
Just then, a shadow fell across me.
A figure was standing just outside the passenger window, blocking the sunlight.
I turned... and recognized the backlit silhouette.
It was... a girl.@k...It was Rika Furude.
She was staring down at me with an expression of listless disinterest.
Without any rational basis for it... I felt as though I had been found by someone I should never have let find me.
...To fill the silence, I raised my hand in an awkward greeting.
Of course, there was no way my small voice could reach her through this wall of noise.
But even so, she should have been able to see me waving.
And yet, the girl...
...simply stared down at me with that emotionless gaze...
At that moment, a fresh wave of stifling heat and noise flooded the car.
Ooishi had returned to the driver's seat.
Looking up, I saw that the group that had been blocking the road was now grudgingly clearing a path, glaring at us all the while.
``Sorry for the wait.@k@vS05/11/100700458. Shall we go?@k@vS05/11/100700459. ...Hm?``
Ooishi noticed the girl's figure past my shoulder.
A villager came running over and scooped the girl up in their arms.
As if to say, 'It's dangerous here -- stay away.'
The girl seemed to murmur a word or two at that moment, but I couldn't hear any of it.
``All right then, let's go.@k@vS05/11/100700461. I'm going to floor it.@k@vS05/11/100700462. Within the legal speed limit, of course.``
As if to shake off the still-audible shouts, Ooishi gunned the accelerator.
The thunderous noise, the angry voices, the villagers, and the girl all shrank away behind us until they vanished.
``Once we get past here, we're in the Takatsudo district.@k@vS05/11/100700464. Ready for a firefight?@k@vS05/11/100700465. Better take the safety off.``
Ooishi laughed teasingly.
...But I was more preoccupied with what the girl had said -- a vague, nagging unease I couldn't shake.
...What had she been trying to say, with that emotionless, detached expression?
``No point in getting worked up now.@k@vS05/11/100700468. There's a real chance this will turn out to be a dead end anyway.``
changeReason: Old translates 収穫がなくて勇み足 as 'do something rash'; new translates as 'turn out to be a dead end.' The Japanese means 'no results and premature action,' which the new captures differently.

TextENGNew: ...There was no longer any way to confirm what the girl had tried to tell me.
That girl who had once warned me to go back to Tokyo -- or else...
...I had ignored her warning and was still here.
``...Even though I warned you. ...What a fool.``
I had the feeling that was what she'd said.
